sections.header: Verses 1–4 — “How Long Will You Forget Me?”

heb.paragraph: "How long (ʿad-ʾānâ), LORD? Will you forget me forever?" (v.1). Four times "how long" in two verses — the most compressed lament in the Psalter. The repetition is desperation: how long forgotten, how long hidden, how long sorrow, how long enemy triumph? The psalm is only six verses but contains the entire lament arc: complaint, petition, trust.

refs.note: "How long, LORD? Will you hide yourself forever?" — the same cry at a national level.
"How long, Sovereign Lord?" — the martyrs’ cry echoes David’s.

notes.note: Four "how long" questions hammer the same point: the wait feels eternal. God seems to have forgotten.
"Look on me and answer. Give light to my eyes, or I will sleep in death." The stakes: without God’s response, David dies.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the four questions create a rhythmic assault on God’s silence. The repetition IS the argument: this has gone on too long.
Calvin: the question "will you forget me forever?" is prayer, not accusation. Calvin: the one who asks God "have you forgotten?" still believes God can remember. It is faith expressed as complaint.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: ʿad-ʾānâ (until when?) appears four times in vv.1–2, creating an anaphoric structure that builds urgency with each repetition.
Kidner: "forever?" is not theology but emotion. David knows God does not forget (Isa 49:15). But suffering FEELS like forever. The psalm legitimises the feeling.
VanGemeren: this is the model lament: brief, honest, structurally complete. The four "how long" questions, the petitions, and the trust-pivot (vv.5–6) in just six verses.
Goldingay: the fourfold "how long" is not patience but urgency. Each repetition escalates: forgotten → hidden face → daily wrestling → enemy triumph.

hist.context: The shortest complete lament. David asks four "how long" questions (vv.1–2), makes two petitions (vv.3–4: look at me, answer me, give light to my eyes lest I sleep in death, lest my enemy triumph). Six verses, complete emotional arc.

sections.header: Verses 5–6 — “I Trust in Your Unfailing Love”

heb.gloss: unfailing love / steadfast love / loyal love
heb.paragraph: "But I trust in your unfailing love (ḥesed); my heart rejoices in your salvation" (v.5). The pivot: from "how long" to "but I trust." Two verses of trust balance four questions of despair. Ḥesed is God’s covenant loyalty — love that endures when feelings fail.

refs.note: "I trust in God’s unfailing love for ever and ever" — the same ḥesed-trust declaration.
"Because of the LORD’s great love we are not consumed" — the same theology in deeper darkness.

notes.note: "But I trust in your unfailing love." The single most important word in lament psalms: "but." Everything before it is complaint; everything after it is faith.
"I will sing the LORD’s praise, for he has been good to me." The psalm ends in song. From tears to music in six verses.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the compression is remarkable: two verses of confidence after four of despair. The disproportion is the point — a little trust outweighs much suffering.
Calvin: "but I trust" is the heart of the Christian life. Calvin: the "but" is not denial of pain but refusal to let pain have the last word.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: ḥesed (covenant love/loyalty) is the Psalter’s most important theological term. It describes God’s self-imposed obligation to love his covenant people. David trusts this, not his feelings.
Kidner: the "but" of v.5 is the hinge of every lament. Without it, the psalm is despair. With it, despair becomes trust. The turn is a choice, not a feeling.
VanGemeren: the shift from lament to praise happens without any change in David’s circumstances. Nothing external changed. The decision to trust changed everything internal.
Goldingay: ḥesed-trust is the bedrock. When feelings say "forgotten," covenant love says "held." The psalm chooses covenant over experience.

hist.context: The pivot is the word "but" (v.5). After four questions and two petitions, David declares trust: "but I trust in your ḥesed." His heart rejoices in God’s salvation. He will sing because God has been good (v.6). The psalm moves from darkness to song in one verse. The shift is not based on changed circumstances but on chosen trust.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: " The stakes: without God’s response, David dies…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVand my enemy will say, “I have overcome him,” and my foes will rejoice when I fall.ESVlest my enemy say, \u201cI have prevailed over him,\u201d lest my foes rejoice because I am shaken.NIVBut I trust in your unfailing love; my heart rejoices in your salvation.ESVBut I have trusted in your steadfast love; my heart shall rejoice in your salvation.

rec.text: "Four "how long" questions hammer the same point: the wait feels eternal. God seems to have forgotten."

rows.label: The Cry — 13:1–4
rows.text: Four "how long?" questions. The compressed lament. "Give light to my eyes or I sleep in death."

hebtext.note: "How long (ʿad-ʾānâ), LORD? Will you forget me forever?" (v.1). Four times "how long" in two verses — the most compressed lament in the Psalter. The repetition is desperation: how long forgotten, how long hidden, how long sorrow, how long enemy triumph? The psalm is only six verses but contains the entire lament arc: complaint, petition, trust.

hebtext.note: "But I trust in your unfailing love (ḥesed); my heart rejoices in your salvation" (v.5). The pivot: from "how long" to "but I trust." Two verses of trust balance four questions of despair. Ḥesed is God’s covenant loyalty — love that endures when feelings fail.

thread.text: "How long, LORD? Will you hide yourself forever?" — the same cry at a national level.
"How long, Sovereign Lord?" — the martyrs’ cry echoes David’s.
"I trust in God’s unfailing love for ever and ever" — the same ḥesed-trust declaration.
"Because of the LORD’s great love we are not consumed" — the same theology in deeper darkness.

themes.note: Faith peaks: the "but I trust" pivot is the essence of lament faith. Mercy: ḥesed is the ground of trust. The perfect six-verse model of complaint → trust.
